is the [land warfare force](/wiki/Army "Army") of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") responsible for defending the UK, the [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories") and [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies"). The British Army, founded in [1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707"), has [seen involvement in most of the world's major wars throughout history](/wiki/Military_history_of_the_United_Kingdom "Military history of the United Kingdom"), including both [world](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") [wars](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). As of 1 January 2025,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit) the British Army comprises 73,847 regular full-time personnel, 4,127 [Gurkhas](/wiki/Brigade_of_Gurkhas "Brigade of Gurkhas"), 25,742 [volunteer reserve](/wiki/Army_Reserve_(United_Kingdom) "Army Reserve (United Kingdom)") personnel and 4,697 "other personnel", for a total of 108,413.[[7]](#cite_note-stats-march-8)

The **British Army** is the [land warfare force](/wiki/Army "Army") of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") responsible for defending the UK, the [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories") and [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies"). The British Army, founded in [1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707"), has [seen involvement in most of the world's major wars throughout history](/wiki/Military_history_of_the_United_Kingdom "Military history of the United Kingdom"), including both [world](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") [wars](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). As of 1 January 2025,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit) the British Army comprises 73,847 regular full-time personnel, 4,127 [Gurkhas](/wiki/Brigade_of_Gurkhas "Brigade of Gurkhas"), 25,742 [volunteer reserve](/wiki/Army_Reserve_(United_Kingdom) "Army Reserve (United Kingdom)") personnel and 4,697 "other personnel", for a total of 108,413.[[7]](#cite_note-stats-march-8)

The British Army traces back to 1707 and the [formation](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") of the united [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") which joined the Kingdoms of [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") and [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") into a [single state](/wiki/Political_union "Political union") and, with that, united the [English Army](/wiki/English_Army "English Army") and the [Scots Army](/wiki/Scots_Army "Scots Army") as the British Army.[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11) The [English](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") [Bill of Rights 1689](/wiki/Bill_of_Rights_1689 "Bill of Rights 1689") and [Scottish](/wiki/Convention_of_the_Estates "Convention of the Estates") [Claim of Right Act 1689](/wiki/Claim_of_Right_Act_1689 "Claim of Right Act 1689") require parliamentary consent for [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") to maintain a peacetime [standing army](/wiki/Standing_army "Standing army").[[11]](#cite_note-12) Members of the British Army swear allegiance to the [monarch](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") as their [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief").[[12]](#cite_note-:2-13) The army is administered by the [Ministry of Defence](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Defence (United Kingdom)") and commanded by the [Chief of the General Staff](/wiki/Chief_of_the_General_Staff_(United_Kingdom) "Chief of the General Staff (United Kingdom)").[[13]](#cite_note-:3-14)

At its inception, being composed primarily of cavalry and infantry, the British Army was one of two *Regular* Forces (there were also separate *Reserve Forces*) within the British military (those parts of the British Armed Forces tasked with land warfare, as opposed to the naval forces),[[14]](#cite_note-15) with the other having been the *Ordnance Military Corps* (made up of the [Royal Artillery](/wiki/Royal_Artillery "Royal Artillery"), [Royal Engineers](/wiki/Royal_Engineers "Royal Engineers"), and the [Royal Sappers and Miners](/wiki/Royal_Sappers_and_Miners "Royal Sappers and Miners")) of the [Board of Ordnance](/wiki/Board_of_Ordnance "Board of Ordnance"), which along with the originally civilian [Commissariat Department](/wiki/Commissariat#19th_century "Commissariat"), stores and supply departments, as well as barracks and other departments, were absorbed into the British Army when the Board of Ordnance was abolished in 1855. Various other civilian departments of the board were absorbed into the [War Office](/wiki/War_Office "War Office").[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18)

The British Army has seen action in major wars between the world's [great powers](/wiki/Great_power "Great power"), including the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"), the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War") and the [First](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and [Second World Wars](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). Britain's victories in most of these decisive wars allowed it to influence world events and establish itself as one of the world's leading [military](/wiki/Military "Military") and [economic](/wiki/Economy "Economy") powers.[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20) Since the end of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), the British Army has been deployed to a number of conflict zones, often as part of an [expeditionary force](/wiki/Expeditionary_warfare "Expeditionary warfare"), a [coalition](/wiki/Coalition "Coalition") force or part of a [United Nations peacekeeping](/wiki/United_Nations_peacekeeping "United Nations peacekeeping") operation.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the British Army](/wiki/History_of_the_British_Army "History of the British Army")

### Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Background")]

Until the [Wars of the Three Kingdoms (1639–1653)](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Wars of the Three Kingdoms"), neither England nor Scotland had a [standing army](/wiki/Standing_army "Standing army") with professional officers and career corporals and sergeants. England relied on [militia](/wiki/Militia_(English) "Militia (English)") organised by local officials or private forces mobilised by the nobility, or on hired mercenaries from Europe.[[21]](#cite_note-22) From the [later Middle Ages](/wiki/Later_Middle_Ages "Later Middle Ages") until the Wars of the Three Kingdoms, when a foreign expeditionary force was needed, such as the one that King [Henry V of England](/wiki/Henry_V_of_England "Henry V of England") took to [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") and that fought at the [Battle of Agincourt](/wiki/Battle_of_Agincourt "Battle of Agincourt") (1415), the army, a professional one, was raised for the duration of the expedition.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Oliver_Cromwell_by_Samuel_Cooper.jpg/250px-Oliver_Cromwell_by_Samuel_Cooper.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oliver_Cromwell_by_Samuel_Cooper.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/General_Thomas_Fairfax_%281612-1671%29_by_Robert_Walker_and_studio.jpg/250px-General_Thomas_Fairfax_%281612-1671%29_by_Robert_Walker_and_studio.jpg)](/wiki/File:General_Thomas_Fairfax_(1612-1671)_by_Robert_Walker_and_studio.jpg)

Lord Protector [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") (left) Lord General [Thomas Fairfax](/wiki/Thomas_Fairfax "Thomas Fairfax"), the first commander of the New Model Army (right)

During the Wars of the Three Kingdoms, the members of the English [Long Parliament](/wiki/Long_Parliament "Long Parliament") realised that the use of county militia organised into regional associations (such as the [Eastern Association](/wiki/Eastern_Association "Eastern Association")), often commanded by local members of Parliament (both from the House of Commons and the House of Lords), while more than able to hold their own in the regions which [Parliamentarians ('Roundheads")](/wiki/Parliamentarians_(English_Civil_War) "Parliamentarians (English Civil War)") controlled, were unlikely to win the war. So Parliament initiated two actions. The [Self-denying Ordinance](/wiki/Self-denying_Ordinance "Self-denying Ordinance") forbade members of Parliament (with the notable exception of [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell"), then a member of parliament and future [Lord Protector](/wiki/Lord_Protector "Lord Protector")) from serving as officers in the Parliamentary armies. This created a distinction between the civilians in Parliament, who tended to be [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") and conciliatory to the Royalists ("Cavaliers") in nature, and a corps of professional officers, who tended to be Independent ([Congregational](/wiki/Congregational "Congregational")) in theology. The second action was legislation for the creation of a Parliamentary-funded army, commanded by Lord General [Thomas Fairfax](/wiki/Thomas_Fairfax "Thomas Fairfax"), which became known as the [New Model Army](/wiki/New_Model_Army "New Model Army") (originally phrased "new-modelled Army").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogers1968207–211-24)

While this proved to be a war-winning formula, the New Model Army, being organised and politically active, went on to dominate the politics of the [Interregnum](/wiki/Interregnum_(England) "Interregnum (England)") and by 1660 was widely disliked. The New Model Army was paid off and disbanded at the later [Restoration](/wiki/Restoration_(1660) "Restoration (1660)") of the monarchy in 1660 with the accession of King [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England"). For many decades the alleged excesses of the New Model Army under [the Protectorate](/wiki/The_Protectorate "The Protectorate") / Commonwealth under Oliver Cromwell were used as propaganda (and still feature in Irish folklore)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÓ_Siochrú20081–2-25) and the [Whig Party](/wiki/Whig_Party_(England) "Whig Party (England)") element recoiled from allowing a standing army to continue with the agreed-upon rights and privileges under the return of a king.[[25]](#cite_note-26) The militia acts of 1661 and 1662 prevented local authorities from calling up militia and oppressing their own local opponents. Calling up the militia was possible only if the king and local elites agreed to do so.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

King Charles II and his "[Cavalier](/wiki/Cavalier "Cavalier")" / Royalist supporters favoured a new army under royal control, and immediately after the Restoration of 1660 to 1661 began working on its establishment.[[27]](#cite_note-28) The first [English Army](/wiki/English_Army#Restoration "English Army") regiments, including elements of the disbanded [New Model Army](/wiki/New_Model_Army "New Model Army"), were formed between November 1660 and January 1661[[28]](#cite_note-29) and became a standing military force for England (financed by [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England")).[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-Clayton2014-31) The [Royal Scots](/wiki/Royal_Scots_Army "Royal Scots Army") and [Irish Armies](/wiki/Irish_Army_(Kingdom_of_Ireland) "Irish Army (Kingdom of Ireland)") were financed by the parliaments of [Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") and [Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland").[[31]](#cite_note-Glozier2007-32) Parliamentary control was established by the [Bill of Rights 1689](/wiki/Bill_of_Rights_1689 "Bill of Rights 1689") and [Claim of Right Act 1689](/wiki/Claim_of_Right_Act_1689 "Claim of Right Act 1689"), although the monarch continued to influence aspects of army administration until at least the end of the 19th century.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

After the Restoration, King Charles II pulled together four regiments of infantry and cavalry, calling them his guards, at a cost of £122,000 from his general budget. This became the foundation of the permanent English Army. By 1685, it had grown to number 7,500 soldiers in marching regiments, and 1,400 men permanently stationed in garrisons. A [Monmouth Rebellion](/wiki/Monmouth_Rebellion "Monmouth Rebellion") in 1685 allowed successor King [James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") to raise the forces to 20,000 men. There were 37,000 in 1678, when England played a role in the closing stage of the cross-channel [Franco-Dutch War](/wiki/Franco-Dutch_War "Franco-Dutch War"). After [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") dual Monarchs [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England"), formerly William of the Dutch [House of Orange](/wiki/House_of_Orange "House of Orange"), and his wife [Mary II's](/wiki/Mary_II_of_England "Mary II of England") joint accession to the throne after a short constitutional crisis with Parliament sending Mary's father, predecessor King James II, (who remained a Catholic) during his brief controversial reign, off the throne and into exile. England then involved itself in the [War of the Grand Alliance](/wiki/War_of_the_Grand_Alliance "War of the Grand Alliance") on the Continent, primarily to prevent a possible French Catholic monarch organizing an invasion restoring the exiled James II (Queen Mary's father and still a [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic")).[[33]](#cite_note-34) Later in 1689, William III to solidify his and Mary's hold on the monarchy, expanded the new English army to 74,000, and then a few years later to 94,000 in 1694. Parliament was very nervous and reduced the cadre to 70,000 in 1697. Scotland and Ireland had theoretically separate military establishments, but they were unofficially merged with the English Crown force.[[34]](#cite_note-35)[[35]](#cite_note-36)

[![Oil-on-canvas portrait](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/John_Churchill_Marlborough_portr%C3%A4tterad_av_Adriaen_van_der_Werff_%281659-1722%29.jpg/250px-John_Churchill_Marlborough_portr%C3%A4tterad_av_Adriaen_van_der_Werff_%281659-1722%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Churchill_Marlborough_portr%C3%A4tterad_av_Adriaen_van_der_Werff_(1659-1722).jpg)

[John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough](/wiki/John_Churchill,_1st_Duke_of_Marlborough "John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough"), was one of the first generals in the new British Army and fought in the War of the Spanish Succession. He was a noted ancestor of Sir [Winston S. Churchill](/wiki/Winston_S._Churchill "Winston S. Churchill"), later famous [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

### Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Formation")]

By the time of the 1707 [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707"), many regiments of the English and Scottish armies were already combined under one operational command and stationed in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") for the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession"). Although all the regiments were now part of the new British military establishment,[[3]](#cite_note-Chandler2003-3) they remained under the old operational-command structure and retained much of the institutional ethos, customs and traditions of the standing armies created shortly after the [Restoration of the Monarchy](/wiki/Restoration_(England) "Restoration (England)") 47 years earlier. The order of seniority of the most-senior British Army line regiments is based on that of the earlier English army. Although technically the Scots [Royal Regiment of Foot](/wiki/Royal_Regiment_of_Foot "Royal Regiment of Foot") was raised in 1633 and is the oldest Regiment of the Line,[[36]](#cite_note-37) Scottish and Irish regiments were only allowed to take a rank in the English army on the date of their arrival in England (or the date when they were first placed on the English establishment). In 1694, a board of general officers was convened to decide the rank of English, Irish and Scots regiments serving in the Netherlands; the regiment which became known as the [Scots Greys](/wiki/Scots_Greys "Scots Greys") were designated the [4th Dragoons](/wiki/4th_Royal_Irish_Dragoon_Guards "4th Royal Irish Dragoon Guards") because there were three English regiments raised prior to 1688 when the Scots Greys were first placed in the English establishment. In 1713, when a new board of general officers was convened to decide the rank of several regiments, the seniority of the Scots Greys was reassessed and based on their June 1685 entry into England. At that time there was only one English regiment of dragoons, and the Scots Greys eventually received the British Army rank of 2nd Dragoons.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoyal_Scots_Greys184056–57-38)

### British Empire (1707–1914)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: British Empire (1707–1914)")]

Main articles: [British Army during the Georgian Era](/wiki/British_Army_during_the_Georgian_Era "British Army during the Georgian Era"), [British Army during the American Revolutionary War](/wiki/British_Army_during_the_American_Revolutionary_War "British Army during the American Revolutionary War"), [British Army during the Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/British_Army_during_the_Napoleonic_Wars "British Army during the Napoleonic Wars"), and [British Army during the Victorian Era](/wiki/British_Army_during_the_Victorian_Era "British Army during the Victorian Era")

After 1707, British continental policy was to contain expansion by competing powers such as France and Spain. Although Spain was the dominant global power during the previous two centuries and the chief threat to England's early trans-Atlantic colonial ambitions, its influence was now waning. The territorial ambitions of the French, however, led to the War of the Spanish Succession[[38]](#cite_note-39) and the later [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars").[[39]](#cite_note-ma165-40)

Although the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") is widely regarded as vital to the rise of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), the British Army played an important role in the formation of colonies, [protectorates](/wiki/Protectorate "Protectorate") and [dominions](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion") in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), [India](/wiki/India "India") and [Australasia](/wiki/Australasia "Australasia").[[40]](#cite_note-41) British soldiers captured strategically important sites and territories, with the army involved in wars to secure the empire's borders, internal safety and support friendly governments and princes. Among these actions were the French and Indian War / Seven Years' War,[[41]](#cite_note-ma106-42) the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"),[[42]](#cite_note-43) the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"),[[39]](#cite_note-ma165-40) the [First](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War") and [Second Opium Wars](/wiki/Second_Opium_War "Second Opium War"),[[43]](#cite_note-ma102-44) the [Boxer Rebellion](/wiki/Boxer_Rebellion "Boxer Rebellion"),[[44]](#cite_note-boxer-45) the [New Zealand Wars](/wiki/New_Zealand_Wars "New Zealand Wars"),[[45]](#cite_note-:0-46) the [Australian frontier wars](/wiki/Australian_frontier_wars "Australian frontier wars"),[[46]](#cite_note-47) the [Sepoy Rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857"),[[47]](#cite_note-ma210-48) the [first](/wiki/First_Boer_War "First Boer War") and [second Boer Wars](/wiki/Second_Boer_War "Second Boer War"),[[48]](#cite_note-ma257-49) the [Fenian raids](/wiki/Fenian_raids "Fenian raids"),[[49]](#cite_note-fenian-50) the [Irish War of Independence](/wiki/Irish_War_of_Independence "Irish War of Independence"),[[50]](#cite_note-ma282-51) interventions in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") (intended to maintain a [buffer state](/wiki/Buffer_state "Buffer state") between [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India") and the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"))[[51]](#cite_note-52) and the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War") (to keep the Russian Empire to the north on the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") at a safe distance by aiding the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire")).[[52]](#cite_note-ma195-53) Like the [English Army](/wiki/English_Army "English Army"), the British Army fought the kingdoms of Spain, France (including the [First French Empire](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire")) and the Netherlands ([Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic")) for supremacy in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") and the [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies "West Indies"). With native and provincial and colonial assistance, the Army conquered [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France") in the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War") (North American theatre) of the parallel [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War")[[41]](#cite_note-ma106-42) and suppressed a [Native / Indian North Americans](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") uprising in [Pontiac's War](/wiki/Pontiac%27s_War "Pontiac's War") around the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes").[[53]](#cite_note-54) The British Army was defeated in the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), losing the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") but retaining [The Canadas](/wiki/The_Canadas "The Canadas") and [The Maritimes](/wiki/The_Maritimes "The Maritimes") as in [British North America](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America"), including [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda") (originally part of the [Colony of Virginia](/wiki/Colony_of_Virginia "Colony of Virginia"), and which had been originally strongly sympathetic to the American colonial rebels early in the war).[[54]](#cite_note-55)

[Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Halifax, Nova Scotia") and [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda") were to become [Imperial fortresses](/wiki/Imperial_fortress "Imperial fortress") (although Bermuda, being safer from attack over water and impervious to attack overland, quickly became the most important in British North America), along with [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta") and [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar"), providing bases in the eastern [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") and [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") for [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") squadrons to control the oceans and trade routes, and heavily garrisoned by the British Army both for defence of the bases and to provide mobile military forces to work with the Navy in amphibious operations throughout their regions.[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60)

[![Panoramic painting of the Battle of Waterloo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Battle_of_Waterloo_1815.PNG/250px-Battle_of_Waterloo_1815.PNG)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_Waterloo_1815.PNG)

The [Duke of Wellington](/wiki/Arthur_Wellesley,_1st_Duke_of_Wellington "Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington") and [Field Marshal von Blücher](/wiki/Field_Marshal_von_Bl%C3%BCcher "Field Marshal von Blücher")'s triumph over [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_I_of_France "Napoleon I of France") at the [Battle of Waterloo](/wiki/The_Battle_of_Waterloo_(painting,_Sadler_II) "The Battle of Waterloo (painting, Sadler II)")

The British Army was heavily involved in the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), participating in a number of campaigns in Europe (including continuous deployment in the [Peninsular War](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War")), the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"), North Africa and [North America](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"). The war between the British and the [First French Empire](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") of [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") stretched around the world; at its peak in 1813, the regular army contained over 250,000 men. A coalition of Anglo-Dutch and Prussian armies under the [Duke of Wellington](/wiki/Duke_of_Wellington "Duke of Wellington") and [Field Marshal von Blücher](/wiki/Field_Marshal_von_Bl%C3%BCcher "Field Marshal von Blücher") finally defeated Napoleon at [Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo") in 1815.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

The English were involved politically and militarily in Ireland. The campaign of English republican Protector [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") involved uncompromising treatment of the Irish towns (most notably [Drogheda](/wiki/Drogheda "Drogheda") and [Wexford](/wiki/Wexford "Wexford")) which supported the Royalists during the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"). The English Army (and the subsequent British Army) remained in Ireland primarily to suppress Irish revolts or disorder. In addition to its conflict with Irish nationalists, it was faced with the prospect of battling Anglo-Irish and [Ulster Scots](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_people "Ulster Scots people") in Ireland who were angered by unfavourable taxation of Irish produce imported into Britain. With other Irish groups, they raised a volunteer army and threatened to emulate the American colonists if their conditions were not met. Learning from their experience in America, the British government sought a political solution. The British Army fought Irish rebels—Protestant and Catholic—primarily in [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster") and [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster "Leinster") ([Wolfe Tone's](/wiki/Theobald_Wolfe_Tone "Theobald Wolfe Tone") [United Irishmen](/wiki/United_Irishmen "United Irishmen")) in the [1798 rebellion](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1798 "Irish Rebellion of 1798").[[61]](#cite_note-62)

[![Painting of the Battle of Rorke's Drift, with a building burning](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Alphonse_de_Neuville_-_The_defence_of_Rorke%27s_Drift_1879_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Alphonse_de_Neuville_-_The_defence_of_Rorke%27s_Drift_1879_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alphonse_de_Neuville_-_The_defence_of_Rorke%27s_Drift_1879_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

In the 1879 [Battle of Rorke's Drift](/wiki/Battle_of_Rorke%27s_Drift "Battle of Rorke's Drift"), a small British force repelled an attack by overwhelming [Zulu](/wiki/Zulu_Empire "Zulu Empire") forces; eleven [Victoria Crosses](/wiki/Victoria_Cross "Victoria Cross") were awarded for its defence.

In addition to battling the armies of other European empires (and its former colonies, the United States, in the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812")),[[62]](#cite_note-63) the British Army fought the Chinese in the First and Second [Opium Wars](/wiki/Opium_Wars "Opium Wars")[[43]](#cite_note-ma102-44) and the [Boxer Rebellion](/wiki/Boxer_Rebellion "Boxer Rebellion"),[[44]](#cite_note-boxer-45) [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") tribes in the first of the New Zealand Wars,[[45]](#cite_note-:0-46) [Nawab Shiraj-ud-Daula's](/wiki/Siraj_ud-Daulah "Siraj ud-Daulah") forces and [British East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company") mutineers in the [Sepoy Rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857"),[[48]](#cite_note-ma257-49) the Boers in the first and second Boer Wars,[[48]](#cite_note-ma257-49) Irish [Fenians](/wiki/Fenian "Fenian") in Canada during the [Fenian raids](/wiki/Fenian_raids "Fenian raids")[[49]](#cite_note-fenian-50) and [Irish separatists](/wiki/Irish_Republican_Army "Irish Republican Army") in the [Anglo-Irish War](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_War "Anglo-Irish War").[[43]](#cite_note-ma102-44) The increasing demands of imperial expansion and the inadequacy and inefficiency of the underfunded British Army, [Militia](/wiki/Militia_(United_Kingdom) "Militia (United Kingdom)"), Ordnance Military Corps, [Yeomanry](/wiki/Yeomanry "Yeomanry") and [Volunteer Force](/wiki/Volunteer_Force_(Great_Britain) "Volunteer Force (Great Britain)") after the Napoleonic Wars led to series of reforms following the failures of the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War").[[63]](#cite_note-64)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Establishment_and_Strength_of_the_British_Army_%28excluding_Indian_native_troops_stationed_in_India%29_prior_to_August%2C_1914.jpg/250px-Establishment_and_Strength_of_the_British_Army_%28excluding_Indian_native_troops_stationed_in_India%29_prior_to_August%2C_1914.jpg)](/wiki/File:Establishment_and_Strength_of_the_British_Army_(excluding_Indian_native_troops_stationed_in_India)_prior_to_August,_1914.jpg)

Establishment and strength of the British Army, excluding Indian native troops stationed in India, prior to August 1914

Inspired by the successes of the [Prussian Army](/wiki/Prussian_Army "Prussian Army") (which relied on short-term conscription of all eligible young men to maintain a large reserve of recently discharged soldiers, ready to be recalled on the outbreak of war to immediately bring the small peacetime regular army up to strength), the [*Regular Reserve*](/wiki/Regular_Reserve_(United_Kingdom)#Army_Reserve_(Regular) "Regular Reserve (United Kingdom)") of the British Army was originally created in 1859 by [Secretary of State for War](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_War "Secretary of State for War") [Sidney Herbert](/wiki/Sidney_Herbert,_1st_Baron_Herbert_of_Lea "Sidney Herbert, 1st Baron Herbert of Lea"), and re-organised under the [Reserve Force Act 1867](/wiki/Reserve_Force_Act_1867 "Reserve Force Act 1867"). Prior to this, a soldier was generally enlisted into the British Army for a 21-year engagement, following which (should he survive so long) he was discharged as a Pensioner. Pensioners were sometimes still employed on garrison duties, as were younger soldiers no longer deemed fit for expeditionary service who were generally organised in invalid units or returned to the regimental depot for home service. The cost of paying pensioners, and the obligation the government was under to continue to employ invalids as well as soldiers deemed by their commanding officers as detriments to their units were motivations to change this system. The long period of engagement also discouraged many potential recruits. The long service enlistments were consequently replaced with short service enlistments, with undesirable soldiers not permitted to re-engage on the completion of their first engagement. The size of the army also fluctuated greatly, increasing in war time, and drastically shrinking with peace. Battalions posted on garrison duty overseas were allowed an increase on their normal peacetime establishment, which resulted in their having surplus men on their return to a *Home* station. Consequently, soldiers engaging on short term enlistments were enabled to serve several years with the colours and the remainder in the Regular Reserve, remaining liable for recall to the colours if required. Among the other benefits, this thereby enabled the British Army to have a ready pool of recently trained men to draw upon in an emergency. The name of the Regular Reserve (which for a time was divided into a *First Class* and a *Second Class*) has resulted in confusion with the *Reserve Forces*, which were the pre-existing part-time, local-service home-defence [forces](/wiki/Units_of_the_British_Army#British_Army_units_in_other_areas_of_the_British_Armed_Forces "Units of the British Army") that were auxiliary to the British Army (or *Regular Force*), but not originally part of it: the [Yeomanry](/wiki/Yeomanry "Yeomanry"), [Militia](/wiki/Militia_(United_Kingdom) "Militia (United Kingdom)") (or *Constitutional Force*) and [Volunteer Force](/wiki/Volunteer_Force "Volunteer Force"). These were consequently also referred to as *Auxiliary Forces* or *Local Forces*.[[64]](#cite_note-65)

The late-19th-century [Cardwell](/wiki/Cardwell_Reforms "Cardwell Reforms") and [Childers Reforms](/wiki/Childers_Reforms "Childers Reforms") gave the army its modern shape and redefined its [regimental system](/wiki/Regimental_system "Regimental system").[[65]](#cite_note-66) The 1907 [Haldane Reforms](/wiki/Haldane_Reforms "Haldane Reforms") created the [Territorial Force](/wiki/Territorial_Force "Territorial Force") as the army's volunteer reserve component, merging and reorganising the Volunteer Force and Yeomanry, while the Militia was replaced by the Special Reserve.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

### World Wars (1914–1945)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: World Wars (1914–1945)")]

Main articles: [British Army during the First World War](/wiki/British_Army_during_the_First_World_War "British Army during the First World War") and [British Army during the Second World War](/wiki/British_Army_during_the_Second_World_War "British Army during the Second World War")

[![Early First World War tank, with soldiers in a trench next to it](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/British_Mark_I_male_tank_Somme_25_September_1916.jpg/250px-British_Mark_I_male_tank_Somme_25_September_1916.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_Mark_I_male_tank_Somme_25_September_1916.jpg)

British First World War [Mark I tank](/wiki/Mark_I_tank "Mark I tank"); the guidance wheels behind the main body were later scrapped as unnecessary. Armoured vehicles of the era required considerable infantry and artillery support. *(Photo by [Ernest Brooks](/wiki/Ernest_Brooks_(photographer) "Ernest Brooks (photographer)"))*

[![Bagpiper leading a line of soldiers though thigh-high growth](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Led_by_their_piper%2C_men_of_7th_Seaforth_Highlanders%2C_15th_%28Scottish%29_Division_advance_during_Operation_%27Epsom%27_in_Normandy%2C_26_June_1944._B6000.jpg/250px-Led_by_their_piper%2C_men_of_7th_Seaforth_Highlanders%2C_15th_%28Scottish%29_Division_advance_during_Operation_%27Epsom%27_in_Normandy%2C_26_June_1944._B6000.jpg)](/wiki/File:Led_by_their_piper,_men_of_7th_Seaforth_Highlanders,_15th_(Scottish)_Division_advance_during_Operation_%27Epsom%27_in_Normandy,_26_June_1944._B6000.jpg)

Led by their piper, men of the 7th Battalion, [Seaforth Highlanders](/wiki/Seaforth_Highlanders "Seaforth Highlanders") (part of the [46th (Highland) Brigade](/wiki/46th_Infantry_Brigade_(United_Kingdom) "46th Infantry Brigade (United Kingdom)")), advance through Normandy during [Operation Epsom](/wiki/Operation_Epsom "Operation Epsom") on 26 June 1944

Great Britain was challenged by other powers, primarily the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") and [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), during the 20th century. A century earlier it vied with Napoleonic France for global pre-eminence, and [Hanoverian](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover") Britain's natural allies were the kingdoms and principalities of [northern Germany](/wiki/Northern_Germany "Northern Germany"). By the middle of the 19th century, Britain and France were allies in preventing Russia's appropriation of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), although the fear of French invasion led shortly afterwards to the creation of the Volunteer Force. By the first decade of the 20th century, the United Kingdom was allied with France (by the [Entente Cordiale](/wiki/Entente_Cordiale "Entente Cordiale")) and Russia (which had a secret agreement with France for mutual support in a war against the [Prussian](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia")-led German Empire and the [Austro-Hungarian Empire](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Empire "Austro-Hungarian Empire")).[[67]](#cite_note-68)

When the First World War broke out in August 1914 the British Army sent the [British Expeditionary Force](/wiki/British_Expeditionary_Force_(World_War_I) "British Expeditionary Force (World War I)") (BEF), consisting mainly of [regular army](/wiki/Standing_army "Standing army") troops, to [France and Belgium](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)").[[68]](#cite_note-69) The fighting bogged down into static [trench warfare](/wiki/Trench_warfare "Trench warfare") for the remainder of the war. In 1915 the army created the [Mediterranean Expeditionary Force](/wiki/Mediterranean_Expeditionary_Force "Mediterranean Expeditionary Force") to invade the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") via [Gallipoli](/wiki/Gallipoli_Campaign "Gallipoli Campaign"), an unsuccessful attempt to capture [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") and secure a sea route to [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire").[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm19113-70)

The First World War was the most devastating in [British military history](/wiki/Military_history_of_the_United_Kingdom "Military history of the United Kingdom"), with nearly 800,000 men killed and over two million wounded. Early in the war, the BEF was virtually destroyed and was replaced first by [volunteers](/wiki/Kitchener%27s_Army "Kitchener's Army") and then by a [conscript](/wiki/Conscription_in_the_United_Kingdom "Conscription in the United Kingdom") force. Major battles included those at [the Somme](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Somme "Battle of the Somme") and [Passchendaele](/wiki/Battle_of_Passchendaele "Battle of Passchendaele").[[70]](#cite_note-71) Advances in technology saw the advent of the [tank](/wiki/Tank "Tank")[[71]](#cite_note-72) (and the creation of the [Royal Tank Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Tank_Regiment "Royal Tank Regiment")) and advances in aircraft design (and the creation of the [Royal Flying Corps](/wiki/Royal_Flying_Corps "Royal Flying Corps")) which would be decisive in future battles.[[72]](#cite_note-73) Trench warfare dominated Western Front strategy for most of the war, and the use of [chemical weapons](/wiki/Chemical_weapons_in_World_War_I "Chemical weapons in World War I") (disabling and poison gases) added to the devastation.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

The [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") broke out in September 1939 with the Soviet and [German Army](/wiki/German_Army_(Wehrmacht) "German Army (Wehrmacht)")'s [invasion of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland").[[74]](#cite_note-ma335-75) British assurances to the Poles led the British Empire to declare war on [Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"). As in the First World War, a relatively small [BEF](/wiki/British_Expeditionary_Force_(World_War_II) "British Expeditionary Force (World War II)") was sent to France[[74]](#cite_note-ma335-75) but then hastily evacuated from [Dunkirk](/wiki/Dunkirk_evacuation "Dunkirk evacuation") as the German forces [swept through the Low Countries and across France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France") in May 1940.[[75]](#cite_note-ma342-76)

After the British Army recovered from its earlier defeats, it defeated the Germans and Italians at the [Second Battle of El Alamein](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_El_Alamein "Second Battle of El Alamein") in [North Africa](/wiki/North_African_Campaign "North African Campaign") in 1942–1943 and helped drive them from Africa. It then fought through [Italy](/wiki/Italian_Campaign_(World_War_II) "Italian Campaign (World War II)")[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1976157-77) and, with the participation of American, Canadian, Australian, New Zealand, Indian and Free French forces,[[77]](#cite_note-78) was one of the principal organisers alongside the U.S. Army and participant in the [D-Day invasion of Normandy](/wiki/Normandy_landings "Normandy landings") on 6 June 1944; nearly half the Allied soldiers were British.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert2005301-79) In the [Far East](/wiki/South-East_Asian_theatre_of_World_War_II "South-East Asian theatre of World War II"), the British Army rallied against the Japanese in the [Burma Campaign](/wiki/Burma_Campaign "Burma Campaign") and regained the British Far Eastern colonial possessions.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1976210-80)

### Postcolonial era (1945–2000)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Postcolonial era (1945–2000)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/1945_Order_of_Precedence_of_the_British_Army.jpg/250px-1945_Order_of_Precedence_of_the_British_Army.jpg)](/wiki/File:1945_Order_of_Precedence_of_the_British_Army.jpg)

1945 Order of Precedence of the British Army

After the Second World War the British Army was significantly reduced in size, although [National Service](/wiki/National_Service "National Service") continued until 1960.[[80]](#cite_note-M_384-81) This period saw [decolonisation](/wiki/Decolonisation "Decolonisation") begin with the [partition](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") and [independence](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement") of India and Pakistan, followed by the independence of British colonies in Africa and Asia.

The [Corps Warrant](/wiki/Corps_Warrant "Corps Warrant"), which is the official list of which bodies of the British Military (not to be confused with *naval*) Forces were to be considered Corps of the British Army for the purposes of the [Army Act](/wiki/Army_Act "Army Act"), the Reserve Forces Act, 1882, and the Territorial and Reserve Forces Act, 1907, had not been updated since 1926 (Army Order 49 of 1926), although amendments had been made up to and including Army Order 67 of 1950. A new Corps Warrant was declared in 1951.

Although the British Army was a major participant in [Korea](/wiki/1st_Commonwealth_Division "1st Commonwealth Division") in the early 1950s[[80]](#cite_note-M_384-81) and [Suez](/wiki/Suez_Crisis "Suez Crisis") in 1956,[[81]](#cite_note-82) during this period Britain's role in world events was reduced and the army was downsized.[[82]](#cite_note-83) The [British Army of the Rhine](/wiki/British_Army_of_the_Rhine "British Army of the Rhine"), consisting of [I (BR) Corps](/wiki/I_Corps_(United_Kingdom) "I Corps (United Kingdom)"), remained in Germany as a bulwark against Soviet invasion.[[83]](#cite_note-84) The [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") continued, with significant technological advances in warfare, and the army saw the introduction of new weapons systems.[[84]](#cite_note-85) Despite the decline of the British Empire, the army was engaged in [Aden](/wiki/Aden_Emergency "Aden Emergency"),[[85]](#cite_note-ma401-86) [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation "Indonesia–Malaysia confrontation"), [Cyprus](/wiki/EOKA "EOKA"),[[85]](#cite_note-ma401-86) [Kenya](/wiki/Mau_Mau_Uprising "Mau Mau Uprising")[[85]](#cite_note-ma401-86) and [Malaya](/wiki/Malayan_Emergency "Malayan Emergency").[[86]](#cite_note-87) In 1982, the British Army and the [Royal Marines](/wiki/Royal_Marines "Royal Marines") helped liberate the [Falkland Islands](/wiki/Falkland_Islands "Falkland Islands") during the [conflict with Argentina](/wiki/Falklands_War "Falklands War") after that country's invasion of the British territory.[[87]](#cite_note-88)

In the three decades following 1969, the army was heavily deployed in [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland")'s [Operation Banner](/wiki/Operation_Banner "Operation Banner") to support the [Royal Ulster Constabulary](/wiki/Royal_Ulster_Constabulary "Royal Ulster Constabulary") (later the [Police Service of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Police_Service_of_Northern_Ireland "Police Service of Northern Ireland")) in their conflict with republican paramilitary groups.[[88]](#cite_note-89) The locally recruited [Ulster Defence Regiment](/wiki/Ulster_Defence_Regiment "Ulster Defence Regiment") was formed, becoming home-service battalions of the [Royal Irish Regiment](/wiki/The_Royal_Irish_Regiment_(27th_(Inniskilling)_83rd_and_87th_and_Ulster_Defence_Regiment) "The Royal Irish Regiment (27th (Inniskilling) 83rd and 87th and Ulster Defence Regiment)") in 1992 before it was disbanded in 2006. Over 700 soldiers were killed during [the Troubles](/wiki/The_Troubles "The Troubles"). Following the 1994–1996 [IRA](/wiki/Provisional_Irish_Republican_Army "Provisional Irish Republican Army") ceasefires and since 1997, demilitarisation has been part of the peace process and the military presence has been reduced.[[89]](#cite_note-90) On 25 June 2007 the 2nd Battalion of the [Princess of Wales's Royal Regiment](/wiki/Princess_of_Wales%27s_Royal_Regiment "Princess of Wales's Royal Regiment") left the army complex in [Bessbrook](/wiki/The_Troubles_in_Bessbrook "The Troubles in Bessbrook"), [County Armagh](/wiki/County_Armagh "County Armagh"), ending the longest operation in British Army history.[[90]](#cite_note-91)

#### Persian Gulf War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Persian Gulf War")]

Main articles: [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War") and [Operation Granby](/wiki/Operation_Granby "Operation Granby")

[![An armoured personnel carrier flying the Union Jack](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/An_APC_of_the_7th_Brigade_Royal_Scots.JPEG/250px-An_APC_of_the_7th_Brigade_Royal_Scots.JPEG)](/wiki/File:An_APC_of_the_7th_Brigade_Royal_Scots.JPEG)

The British Army contributed 50,000 troops to the coalition which fought [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") in the [Persian Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War"),[[91]](#cite_note-92) and British forces controlled [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait") after its liberation. Forty-seven British military personnel died during the war.[[92]](#cite_note-93)

#### Balkan conflicts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Balkan conflicts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/British_Army_vehicles_in_Croatia%2C_1996.jpg/250px-British_Army_vehicles_in_Croatia%2C_1996.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_Army_vehicles_in_Croatia,_1996.jpg)

British Army vehicles in a staging area before being deployed to Bosnia

Main article: [Yugoslav Wars](/wiki/Yugoslav_Wars "Yugoslav Wars")

The army was deployed to former [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia") in 1992. Initially part of the [United Nations Protection Force](/wiki/United_Nations_Protection_Force "United Nations Protection Force"),[[93]](#cite_note-94) in 1995 its command was transferred to the [Implementation Force](/wiki/Implementation_Force "Implementation Force") (IFOR) and then to the [Stabilisation Force in Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Stabilisation_Force_in_Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Stabilisation Force in Bosnia and Herzegovina") (SFOR);[[94]](#cite_note-95) the commitment rose to over 10,000 troops. In 1999, British forces under SFOR command were sent to [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo") and the contingent increased to 19,000 troops.[[95]](#cite_note-96) Between early 1993 and June 2010, 72 British military personnel died during operations in the former Yugoslavian countries of Bosnia, Kosovo and Macedonia.[[96]](#cite_note-:6-97)

#### The Troubles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: The Troubles")]

Although there have been permanent garrisons in Northern Ireland throughout its history, the British Army was deployed as a peacekeeping force from 1969 to 2007 in [Operation Banner](/wiki/Operation_Banner "Operation Banner").[[97]](#cite_note-98) Initially, this was (in the wake of [unionist](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland") attacks on nationalist communities in [Derry](/wiki/Derry "Derry")[[98]](#cite_note-99) and [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast"))[[99]](#cite_note-100) to prevent further loyalist attacks on Catholic communities; it developed into support of the [Royal Ulster Constabulary](/wiki/Royal_Ulster_Constabulary "Royal Ulster Constabulary") (RUC) and its successor, the [Police Service of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Police_Service_of_Northern_Ireland "Police Service of Northern Ireland") (PSNI) against the [Provisional Irish Republican Army](/wiki/Provisional_Irish_Republican_Army "Provisional Irish Republican Army") (PIRA).[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKernan200517-101) Under the 1998 [Good Friday Agreement](/wiki/Good_Friday_Agreement "Good Friday Agreement"), there was a gradual reduction in the number of soldiers deployed.[[101]](#cite_note-102) In 2005, after the PIRA declared a ceasefire, the British Army dismantled posts, withdrew many troops and restored troop levels to those of a peacetime garrison.[[102]](#cite_note-103)

Operation Banner ended at midnight on 31 July 2007 after about 38 years of continuous deployment, the longest in British Army history.[[103]](#cite_note-analysis-104) According to an internal document released in 2007, the British Army had failed to defeat the IRA but made it impossible for them to win by violence. Operation Helvetic replaced Operation Banner in 2007, maintaining fewer service personnel in a more-benign environment.[[103]](#cite_note-analysis-104)[[104]](#cite_note-BBC-105) Of the 300,000 troops who served in Northern Ireland since 1969, there were 763 British military personnel killed[[105]](#cite_note-106) and 306 killed by the British military, mostly civilians.[[106]](#cite_note-107) An estimated 100 soldiers committed suicide during Operation Banner or soon afterwards and a similar number died in accidents. A total of 6,116 were wounded.[[107]](#cite_note-108)

**Sierra Leone**

Main article: [British military intervention in the Sierra Leone Civil War](/wiki/British_military_intervention_in_the_Sierra_Leone_Civil_War "British military intervention in the Sierra Leone Civil War")

The British Army deployed to Sierra Leone for [Operation Palliser](/wiki/Operation_Palliser "Operation Palliser") in 1999, under United Nations resolutions, to aid the government in quelling violent uprisings by militiamen. British troops also provided support during the 2014 [West African Ebola virus epidemic](/wiki/West_African_Ebola_virus_epidemic "West African Ebola virus epidemic").[[108]](#cite_note-109)

### Recent history (2000–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Recent history (2000–present)")]

#### War in Afghanistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: War in Afghanistan")]

Main article: [War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)")

[![Armed soldiers in and around a military vehicle](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/3royalanglianafghan.JPG/250px-3royalanglianafghan.JPG)](/wiki/File:3royalanglianafghan.JPG)

[Royal Anglian Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Anglian_Regiment "Royal Anglian Regiment") in Helmand Province

In November 2001, as part of [Operation Enduring Freedom](/wiki/Operation_Enduring_Freedom "Operation Enduring Freedom") with the United States, the United Kingdom deployed forces in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") to topple the [Taliban](/wiki/Taliban "Taliban") in [Operation Herrick](/wiki/Operation_Herrick "Operation Herrick").[[109]](#cite_note-110) The [3rd Division](/wiki/3rd_Division_(United_Kingdom) "3rd Division (United Kingdom)") were sent to [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul") to assist in the liberation of the capital and defeat Taliban forces in the mountains. In 2006 the British Army began concentrating on fighting Taliban forces and bringing security to [Helmand Province](/wiki/Helmand_Province "Helmand Province"), with about 9,500 British troops (including marines, airmen and sailors) deployed at its peak[[110]](#cite_note-111)—the second-largest force after that of the US.[[111]](#cite_note-112) In December 2012 Prime Minister [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") announced that the combat mission would end in 2014, and troop numbers gradually fell as the [Afghan National Army](/wiki/Afghan_National_Army "Afghan National Army") took over the brunt of the fighting. Between 2001 and 26 April 2014 a total of 453 British military personnel died in Afghan operations.[[112]](#cite_note-113) Operation Herrick ended with the handover of [Camp Bastion](/wiki/Camp_Bastion "Camp Bastion") on 26 October 2014,[[113]](#cite_note-114) but the British Army maintained a deployment in Afghanistan as part of [Operation Toral](/wiki/Operation_Toral "Operation Toral").[[114]](#cite_note-toral-115)

Following an announcement by the US Government of the end of their operations in the Afghanistan, the Ministry of Defence announced in April 2021 that British forces would withdraw from the country by 11 September 2021.[[115]](#cite_note-116) It was later reported that all UK troops would be out by early July.[[116]](#cite_note-auto-117) Following the collapse of the Afghan Army, and the completion of the withdrawal of civilians, all British troops had left by the end of August 2021.[[117]](#cite_note-118)

#### Iraq War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Iraq War")]

Main articles: [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War") and [Operation Telic](/wiki/Operation_Telic "Operation Telic")

[![Two soldiers with a mortar gun—one loading and the other aiming](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/1_RRF_engage_Iraqi_Army_positions_with_their_81mm_Mortars._Iraq._26-03-2003_MOD_45142764.jpg/250px-1_RRF_engage_Iraqi_Army_positions_with_their_81mm_Mortars._Iraq._26-03-2003_MOD_45142764.jpg)](/wiki/File:1_RRF_engage_Iraqi_Army_positions_with_their_81mm_Mortars._Iraq._26-03-2003_MOD_45142764.jpg)

British soldiers from the [1st Battalion, Royal Regiment of Fusiliers](/wiki/Royal_Regiment_of_Fusiliers "Royal Regiment of Fusiliers") [battlegroup](/wiki/Battlegroup_(army) "Battlegroup (army)") engage Iraqi positions with an [81mm mortar](/wiki/81mm_mortar "81mm mortar") [south of Basra](/wiki/Battle_of_Basra_(2003) "Battle of Basra (2003)")

In 2003, the United Kingdom was a major contributor to the [invasion of Iraq](/wiki/2003_invasion_of_Iraq "2003 invasion of Iraq"), sending a force of over 46,000 military personnel. The British Army controlled southern Iraq, and maintained a peace-keeping presence in [Basra](/wiki/Basra "Basra").[[118]](#cite_note-119) All British troops were withdrawn from Iraq by 30 April 2009, after the Iraqi government refused to extend their mandate.[[119]](#cite_note-120) One hundred and seventy-nine British military personnel died in Iraqi operations.[[96]](#cite_note-:6-97) The [British Armed Forces](/wiki/British_Armed_Forces "British Armed Forces") returned to Iraq in 2014 as part of [Operation Shader](/wiki/Operation_Shader "Operation Shader") to counter the [Islamic State](/wiki/Islamic_State_of_Iraq_and_the_Levant "Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant") (ISIL).[[120]](#cite_note-121)

#### Recent military aid

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Recent military aid")]

Main articles: [Operation Rescript](/wiki/Operation_Rescript "Operation Rescript"), [Operation Temperer](/wiki/Operation_Temperer "Operation Temperer"), and [Military Aid to the Civil Authorities](/wiki/Military_Aid_to_the_Civil_Authorities "Military Aid to the Civil Authorities")

The British Army maintains a standing liability to support the civil authorities in certain circumstances, usually in either niche capabilities (e.g. explosive ordnance removal) or in general support of the civil authorities when their capacity is exceeded.[[121]](#cite_note-122)[[122]](#cite_note-123) In recent years this has been seen as army personnel supporting the civil authorities in the face of the [2001 United Kingdom foot-and-mouth outbreak](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_foot-and-mouth_outbreak "2001 United Kingdom foot-and-mouth outbreak"), the 2002 firefighters strike, widespread flooding in 2005, 2007, 2009, 2013 and 2014, [Operation Temperer](/wiki/Operation_Temperer "Operation Temperer") following the [Manchester Arena bombing](/wiki/Manchester_Arena_bombing "Manchester Arena bombing") in 2017 and, most recently, [Operation Rescript](/wiki/Operation_Rescript "Operation Rescript") during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[123]](#cite_note-124)[[124]](#cite_note-OpRESCRIPT-125)

#### Baltic states

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Baltic states")]

Since 2016, the British Army has maintained a presence in the [Baltic states](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states") in support of the [NATO Enhanced Forward Presence](/wiki/NATO_Enhanced_Forward_Presence "NATO Enhanced Forward Presence") strategy which responded to the [2014 Russian annexation of Crimea](/wiki/2014_Russian_annexation_of_Crimea "2014 Russian annexation of Crimea"). The British Army leads a multinational armoured battlegroup in [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia") under Operation Cabrit and contributes troops to another military battle group in [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland").[[125]](#cite_note-efp-126) As part of the NATO plans, Britain has committed a full mechanized infantry [brigade](/wiki/Brigade "Brigade") to be on a high state of readiness to defend Estonia.[[126]](#cite_note-127)

#### Ukraine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Ukraine")]

Between 2015 and 2022, the British Army deployed Short Term Training Teams (STTTs) to [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") under [Operation Orbital](/wiki/Operation_Orbital "Operation Orbital") to help train the [Armed Forces of Ukraine](/wiki/Armed_Forces_of_Ukraine "Armed Forces of Ukraine") against further Russian aggression.[[127]](#cite_note-128) This operation was succeeded by [Operation Interflex](/wiki/Operation_Interflex "Operation Interflex") in July 2022.[[128]](#cite_note-politico1-129)

## Modern army

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Modern army")]

### Personnel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Personnel")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Trooping_the_Colour_MOD_45155754.jpg/250px-Trooping_the_Colour_MOD_45155754.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trooping_the_Colour_MOD_45155754.jpg)

The [Blues and Royals](/wiki/Blues_and_Royals "Blues and Royals") [Trooping the Colour](/wiki/Trooping_the_Colour "Trooping the Colour") in 2013

The British Army has been a volunteer force since national service ended during the 1960s.[[80]](#cite_note-M_384-81) Since the creation of the part-time, reserve [Territorial Force](/wiki/Territorial_Force "Territorial Force") in 1908 (renamed the Territorial Army in 1921 and the Army Reserve in 2014), the full-time British Army has been known as the Regular Army. In July 2020 there were just over 78,800 Regulars, with a target strength of 82,000, and just over 30,000 [Army Reservists](/wiki/Army_Reserve_(United_Kingdom) "Army Reserve (United Kingdom)"), with a target strength of 30,000.[[129]](#cite_note-statistics2021-130) All former Regular Army personnel may also be recalled to duty in exceptional circumstances during the 6-year period following completion of their Regular service, which creates an additional force known as the [Regular Reserve](/wiki/Regular_Reserve_(United_Kingdom)#Army_Reserve_(Regular) "Regular Reserve (United Kingdom)").[[130]](#cite_note-131)

As of January 2025, the British Army had 73,847 regular soldiers, 4,127 Gurkhas, and 25,742 volunteer reserve personnel.[[6]](#cite_note-stats-7) Under the recommendations of the 2025 [Strategic Defence Review](/wiki/Strategic_Defence_Review_(2025) "Strategic Defence Review (2025)"), the number of regular soldiers is planned to stop declining and eventually increase to 76,000.[[131]](#cite_note-132)

## Equipment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Equipment")]

Main article: [List of equipment of the British Army](/wiki/List_of_equipment_of_the_British_Army "List of equipment of the British Army")

### Infantry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Infantry")]

The British Army's basic weapon is the 5.56 mm [L85A2 or L85A3](/wiki/SA80 "SA80") assault rifle, with some specialist personnel using the L22A2 carbine variant (pilots and some tank crew). The weapon was traditionally equipped with either [iron sights](/wiki/Iron_sights "Iron sights") or an optical [SUSAT](/wiki/SUSAT "SUSAT"), although other optical sights have been subsequently purchased to supplement these.[[150]](#cite_note-151) The weapon can be enhanced further utilising the [Picatinny rail](/wiki/Picatinny_rail "Picatinny rail") with attachments such as the [L17A2](/wiki/L17A2_UGL "L17A2 UGL") under-barrel grenade launcher.[[151]](#cite_note-:8-152) In 2023, the [Army Special Operations Brigade](/wiki/Army_Special_Operations_Brigade "Army Special Operations Brigade"), which includes the [Ranger Regiment](/wiki/Ranger_Regiment_(United_Kingdom) "Ranger Regiment (United Kingdom)"), began using the [L403A1](/wiki/L403A1 "L403A1"), an [AR-pattern](/wiki/M16_rifle "M16 rifle") rifle also used by the [Royal Marines](/wiki/Royal_Marines "Royal Marines").[[152]](#cite_note-153) An initiative to find a replacement for the SA80 family, known as [Project Grayburn](/wiki/Project_Grayburn "Project Grayburn"), was in the concept phase in 2025,[[153]](#cite_note-154) and is expected to enter the assessment phase in 2026.[[154]](#cite_note-155)

Some soldiers are equipped with the 7.62mm [L129A1 sharpshooter rifle](/wiki/L129A1 "L129A1"),[[155]](#cite_note-156) which in 2018 formally replaced the [L86A2](/wiki/L86_LSW "L86 LSW") Light Support Weapon. Support fire is provided by the [L7 general-purpose machine gun](/wiki/L7_(machine_gun) "L7 (machine gun)") (GPMG),[[156]](#cite_note-157) and indirect fire is provided by [L16 81mm mortars](/wiki/L16_81mm_mortar "L16 81mm mortar"). Sniper rifles include the [L118A1](/wiki/Accuracy_International_Arctic_Warfare "Accuracy International Arctic Warfare") 7.62 mm, [L115A3](/wiki/L115A3 "L115A3") and the [AW50F](/wiki/AW50F "AW50F"), all manufactured by [Accuracy International](/wiki/Accuracy_International "Accuracy International").[[157]](#cite_note-158) The British Army utilises the [Glock 17](/wiki/Glock "Glock") as its side arm.[[151]](#cite_note-:8-152)

Anti tank guided weapons include the [Javelin](/wiki/FGM-148_Javelin "FGM-148 Javelin"), the medium range anti-tank guided weapon replacement for [Milan](/wiki/MILAN "MILAN"), with overfly and direct attack modes of operation, and the [NLAW](/wiki/NLAW "NLAW"). The Next-generation Light Anti-tank Weapon ([NLAW](/wiki/NLAW "NLAW")) is the first, non-expert, short-range, anti-tank missile that rapidly knocks out any main battle tank in just one shot by striking it from above.[[158]](#cite_note-159)

### Armour

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Armour")]

The army's [main battle tank](/wiki/Main_battle_tank "Main battle tank") is the [Challenger 2](/wiki/Challenger_2 "Challenger 2"), which is being upgraded to [Challenger 3](/wiki/Challenger_3 "Challenger 3").[[159]](#cite_note-160)[[160]](#cite_note-161) It is supported by the [Warrior tracked armoured vehicle](/wiki/Warrior_tracked_armoured_vehicle "Warrior tracked armoured vehicle") as the primary [infantry fighting vehicle](/wiki/Infantry_fighting_vehicle "Infantry fighting vehicle"),[[161]](#cite_note-162) (which will soon be replaced by the [Boxer 8x8 armoured fighting vehicle](/wiki/Boxer_(armoured_fighting_vehicle) "Boxer (armoured fighting vehicle)")) and the [Bulldog armoured personnel carrier](/wiki/FV430_series "FV430 series").[[162]](#cite_note-163) The [Ajax armoured fighting vehicle](/wiki/General_Dynamics_Ajax "General Dynamics Ajax") is also being brought into service. Light armoured units often utilise the [Supacat "Jackal" MWMIK](/wiki/MWMIK "MWMIK") and [Coyote](/wiki/Jackal_(vehicle)#Coyote "Jackal (vehicle)") tactical support vehicle for reconnaissance and fire support.[[163]](#cite_note-164)

### Artillery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Artillery")]

The army has three main artillery systems: the [M270 multiple launch rocket system](/wiki/M270_multiple_launch_rocket_system "M270 multiple launch rocket system") (MLRS), the [Archer](/wiki/Archer_artillery_system "Archer artillery system") and the [L118 light gun](/wiki/L118_light_gun "L118 light gun").[[164]](#cite_note-165) The MLRS, first used in [Operation Granby](/wiki/Operation_Granby "Operation Granby"), has an 85-kilometre (53 mi) standard range, or with the [PrSM](/wiki/Precision_Strike_Missile "Precision Strike Missile"), up to 500 km.[[165]](#cite_note-166) The Archer is a 155 mm self-propelled armoured gun with a 50-kilometre (31 mi) range. The L118 light gun is a 105 mm towed gun, which is typically towed by a [Pinzgauer](/wiki/Steyr-Puch_Pinzgauer "Steyr-Puch Pinzgauer") all-terrain vehicle.[[166]](#cite_note-167) The army plans to replace the Archer with the [RCH 155](/wiki/RCH_155 "RCH 155") in the near future.[[167]](#cite_note-168)

To identify artillery targets, the army operates the TAIPAN artillery detection radar[[168]](#cite_note-169) and utilises [artillery sound ranging](/wiki/Artillery_sound_ranging "Artillery sound ranging").[[169]](#cite_note-170) For air defence it uses the new [Sky Sabre](/wiki/Sky_Sabre "Sky Sabre") system, which in 2021 replaced the [Rapier](/wiki/Rapier_(missile) "Rapier (missile)").[[170]](#cite_note-whatdotheyknow.com-171) It also deploys the Very Short-Range Air Defence (VSHORAD) [Starstreak HVM](/wiki/Starstreak_(missile) "Starstreak (missile)") (high-velocity missile) launched by a single soldier or from a [Stormer HVM](/wiki/Alvis_Stormer "Alvis Stormer") vehicle-mounted launcher.[[171]](#cite_note-172)

### Protected mobility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Protected mobility")]

Where armour is not required or mobility and speed are favoured the British Army utilises protected patrol vehicles, such as the Panther variant of the [Iveco LMV](/wiki/Iveco_LMV "Iveco LMV"), the [Foxhound](/wiki/Ocelot_(vehicle) "Ocelot (vehicle)"), and variants of the [Cougar](/wiki/Cougar_(MRAP) "Cougar (MRAP)") family (such as the Ridgeback, Husky and Mastiff).[[172]](#cite_note-173) For day-to-day utility work the army commonly uses the [Land Rover Wolf](/wiki/Land_Rover_Wolf "Land Rover Wolf"), which is based on the [Land Rover Defender](/wiki/Land_Rover_Defender "Land Rover Defender").[[173]](#cite_note-174)

### Engineers, utility and signals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Engineers, utility and signals")]

Specialist engineering vehicles include bomb-disposal robots such as the [T7 Multi-Mission Robotic System](/wiki/L3Harris "L3Harris") and the modern variants of the [Armoured Vehicle Royal Engineers](/wiki/Armoured_Vehicle_Royal_Engineers "Armoured Vehicle Royal Engineers"), including the Titan bridge-layer, [Trojan](/wiki/Trojan_Armoured_Vehicle_Royal_Engineers "Trojan Armoured Vehicle Royal Engineers") armoured engineer vehicle, [Terrier armoured digger](/wiki/Terrier_armoured_digger "Terrier armoured digger").[[174]](#cite_note-175) Day-to-day utility work uses a series of support vehicles, including six-, nine- and fifteen-tonne [MAN](/wiki/MAN_Truck_%26_Bus "MAN Truck & Bus") trucks, [Oshkosh](/wiki/Oshkosh_Corporation "Oshkosh Corporation") heavy-equipment transporters (HET), close-support tankers, quad bikes and ambulances.[[175]](#cite_note-176)[[176]](#cite_note-177) Tactical communication uses the [Bowman](/wiki/Bowman_(communications_system) "Bowman (communications system)") radio system, and operational or strategic communication is controlled by the [Royal Corps of Signals](/wiki/Royal_Corps_of_Signals "Royal Corps of Signals").[[177]](#cite_note-178)

### Aviation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Aviation")]

The [Army Air Corps](/wiki/British_Army_Air_Corps "British Army Air Corps") (AAC) provides direct aviation support, with the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") providing support helicopters. The primary attack helicopter is the [Boeing AH-64E Apache](/wiki/AH-64E_Apache "AH-64E Apache") which replaced the [AgustaWestland Apache AH-1](/wiki/AgustaWestland_Apache "AgustaWestland Apache") in the anti-tank, anti-air defence, and anti-armour role.[[178]](#cite_note-179) The [AgustaWestland AW159 Wildcat](/wiki/AgustaWestland_AW159_Wildcat "AgustaWestland AW159 Wildcat") is a dedicated [intelligence, surveillance, target acquisition, and reconnaissance](/wiki/Intelligence,_surveillance,_target_acquisition,_and_reconnaissance "Intelligence, surveillance, target acquisition, and reconnaissance") (ISTAR) helicopter.[[179]](#cite_note-180) The [Eurocopter AS 365N Dauphin](/wiki/Eurocopter_Dauphin "Eurocopter Dauphin") is used for [special operations](/wiki/Special_operations "Special operations") aviation, primarily [counter terrorism](/wiki/Counterterrorism "Counterterrorism") operations, within the UK.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERipley200810-181) The army operates [unmanned aerial vehicles](/wiki/Unmanned_aerial_vehicle "Unmanned aerial vehicle") in a surveillance role, such as the small [Lockheed Martin Desert Hawk III](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin_Desert_Hawk_III "Lockheed Martin Desert Hawk III").[[181]](#cite_note-182)[[182]](#cite_note-183)

British soldiers are not in a combat role in Iraq but are on the ground with coalition partners providing training and equipment to Iraqi Security Forces (ISF) and Kurdish Security Forces (KSF). There were approximately 400 military personnel in Iraq in 2020.[[183]](#cite_note-184) |
| [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") | Since 1964 | [Operation Tosca](/wiki/United_Nations_Peacekeeping_Force_in_Cyprus "United Nations Peacekeeping Force in Cyprus"): There were 275 troops deployed as part of the UNFICYP in 2016.[[184]](#cite_note-org-185) |
| [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia") | Since 2017 | [NATO Enhanced Forward Presence](/wiki/NATO_Enhanced_Forward_Presence "NATO Enhanced Forward Presence"): The British Army deploys approximately 900 troops to Estonia and 150 to Poland as part of its commitment to NATO.[[185]](#cite_note-186) |
| [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") | Since 2019 | The British Army maintains several short-term military training teams to help build the capacity of national military forces, ensuring a number of states across Africa can respond appropriately and proportionally to the security threats they face, including terrorism, the illegal wildlife trade, violations of human rights and emerging humanitarian crises.[[146]](#cite_note-:1-147) |

### Permanent overseas postings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Permanent overseas postings")]

| Location | Date | Details |

| [Belize](/wiki/Belize "Belize") | 1949 | [British Army Training and Support Unit Belize](/wiki/British_Army_Training_and_Support_Unit_Belize "British Army Training and Support Unit Belize"): The British Army has maintained a presence in Belize since its independence. Currently the British Army Training Support Unit in Belize enables close country and tropical environment training to troops from the UK and international partners.[[186]](#cite_note-187) |
| [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda") | 1701 | [Royal Bermuda Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Bermuda_Regiment "Royal Bermuda Regiment"): Colonial Militia and volunteers existed from 1612 to 1816. The regular English Army, then British Army, [Bermuda Garrison](/wiki/Bermuda_Garrison "Bermuda Garrison") was first established by an [Independent Company](/wiki/Independent_Company "Independent Company") in 1701.[[187]](#cite_note-188) Volunteers were recruited into the regular army and the [Board of Ordnance](/wiki/Board_of_Ordnance "Board of Ordnance") Military Corps for part-time, local-service from the 1830s to the 1850s due to the lack of a Militia. The British Government considered Bermuda as an [Imperial fortress](/wiki/Imperial_fortress "Imperial fortress"), rather than a colony. After the French Revolution, the [Governor of Bermuda](/wiki/Governor_of_Bermuda "Governor of Bermuda") was normally a military officer (usually a Lieutenant-General or Major-General of the Royal Artillery or Royal Engineers) in charge of all military forces in Bermuda, with the Bermuda Garrison falling under the Nova Scotia Command. From 1868, the Bermuda Garrison became the independent Bermuda Command, with Governors being Generals, Lieutenant-Generals or Major Generals occupying the role of Commander-in-Chief or [General Officer Commanding-in-Chief](/wiki/General_officer_commanding#GOC-in-C "General officer commanding") (GOC-in-C). Locally recruited reserve units, the Royal Artillery-badged [Bermuda Militia Artillery](/wiki/Bermuda_Militia_Artillery "Bermuda Militia Artillery") (BMA) and [Bermuda Volunteer Rifle Corps](/wiki/Bermuda_Volunteer_Rifle_Corps "Bermuda Volunteer Rifle Corps") (BVRC) were raised again from 1894, later joined by the Royal Engineers-badged [Bermuda Volunteer Engineers](/wiki/Bermuda_Volunteer_Engineers "Bermuda Volunteer Engineers") (1931–1946), [General Service Corps](/wiki/General_Service_Corps "General Service Corps")-badged [Bermuda Militia Infantry](/wiki/Bermuda_Militia_Infantry "Bermuda Militia Infantry") (1939–1946), and a Home Guard (1942–1946). After the [Royal Naval Dockyard](/wiki/Royal_Naval_Dockyard,_Bermuda "Royal Naval Dockyard, Bermuda") was redesignated a naval base in 1951, the army garrison was closed in 1957, leaving only the part-time BMA (re-tasked as infantry in 1953, though still badged and uniformed as Royal Artillery) and BVRC (renamed Bermuda Rifles in 1949). The Bermuda Command Headquarters and all regular army personnel other than members of the Permanent Staff of the local Territorials and the [Aide-de-Camp](/wiki/Aide-de-Camp "Aide-de-Camp") to the Governor of Bermuda (today normally a Captain from the [Royal Bermuda Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Bermuda_Regiment "Royal Bermuda Regiment") employed full-time for the duration of the appointment) were withdrawn. Home defence has been provided by the [Royal Bermuda Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Bermuda_Regiment "Royal Bermuda Regiment") since formed by the 1965 amalgamation of the BMA and Bermuda Rifles.[[188]](#cite_note-189) |
| [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei") | 1962 | [British Forces Brunei](/wiki/British_Forces_Brunei "British Forces Brunei"): One battalion of the [Royal Gurkha Rifles](/wiki/Royal_Gurkha_Rifles "Royal Gurkha Rifles"), [British Garrison](/wiki/British_Military_Garrison_Brunei "British Military Garrison Brunei"), [Training Team Brunei (TTB)](/wiki/British_Army_Jungle_Warfare_Training_School "British Army Jungle Warfare Training School"). A Gurkha battalion has been maintained in Brunei since the [Brunei Revolt](/wiki/Brunei_Revolt "Brunei Revolt") in 1962 at the request of [Sultan](/wiki/List_of_Sultans_of_Brunei "List of Sultans of Brunei") [Omar Ali Saifuddin III](/wiki/Omar_Ali_Saifuddin_III "Omar Ali Saifuddin III"). Training Team Brunei (TTB) is the Army's jungle-warfare school, and a small number of garrison troops support the battalion. 7 Flight AAC formerly provided helicopter support to the Gurkha battalion and TTB[[189]](#cite_note-Brunei-190) but its role has since been assumed by [No. 230 Squadron RAF](/wiki/No._230_Squadron_RAF "No. 230 Squadron RAF"). |
| [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") | 1972 | [British Army Training Unit Suffield](/wiki/British_Army_Training_Unit_Suffield "British Army Training Unit Suffield"): A training centre in [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta") prairie for the use of British Army and [Canadian Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Forces "Canadian Forces") under agreement with the [government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada"). British forces conduct regular, major armoured training exercises every year, with helicopter support provided by 29 (BATUS) Flight [AAC](/wiki/Army_Air_Corps_(United_Kingdom) "Army Air Corps (United Kingdom)").[[190]](#cite_note-191)[[191]](#cite_note-29_BATUS_AAC-192) |
| [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") | 1960 | 2 resident infantry battalions, [Royal Engineers](/wiki/Royal_Engineers "Royal Engineers") and Joint Service Signals Unit at [Ayios Nikolaos](/wiki/Ayios_Nikolaos_Station "Ayios Nikolaos Station") as part of [British Forces Cyprus](/wiki/British_Forces_Cyprus "British Forces Cyprus"). The UK retains two [Sovereign Base Areas](/wiki/Sovereign_Base_Areas "Sovereign Base Areas") on Cyprus after the rest of the island's independence, which are forward bases for deployments to the Middle East. Principal facilities are Alexander Barracks at Dhekelia and Salamanca Barracks at [Episkopi](/wiki/Episkopi,_Limassol "Episkopi, Limassol").[[192]](#cite_note-193) |
| [Falkland Islands](/wiki/Falkland_Islands "Falkland Islands") | 1982 | Part of [British Forces South Atlantic Islands](/wiki/British_Forces_South_Atlantic_Islands "British Forces South Atlantic Islands"): After the 1982 conflict, the UK established a garrison on the Falkland Islands, consisting of naval, land and air elements. The British Army contribution consists of an infantry company group, a Royal Artillery Battery and an Engineer Squadron.[[193]](#cite_note-194) |

The people of Gibraltar took up arms as the Gibraltar Volunteer Corps from 1915 to 1920 and again as the Gibraltar Defence Force shortly before the outbreak of WW2. This force later became the [Royal Gibraltar Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Gibraltar_Regiment "Royal Gibraltar Regiment"), which remains as the only formed Army unit in Gibraltar.[[194]](#cite_note-195) |

BATUK provides demanding training to exercising units preparing to deploy on operations or assume high-readiness tasks. BATUK consists of around 100 permanent staff and reinforcing short tour cohort of another 280 personnel. Under an agreement with the Kenyan Government, up to six infantry battalions per year carry out eight-week exercises in Kenya.[[146]](#cite_note-:1-147) There are also Royal Engineer exercises, which carry out civil engineering projects, and medical deployments, which provide primary health care assistance to the civilian community under an agreement with the Kenyan government, which provides training facilities for 3 infantry battalions per year.[[195]](#cite_note-Africa-196) |
