Main articles: [for the current organisation](/wiki/Structure_of_the_British_Army "Structure of the British Army") and [for the Future Soldier organisation](/wiki/Future_Soldier_(British_Army) "Future Soldier (British Army)")

[Army Headquarters](/wiki/Army_Headquarters_(United_Kingdom) "Army Headquarters (United Kingdom)") is located in [Andover, Hampshire](/wiki/Andover,_Hampshire "Andover, Hampshire"), and is responsible for providing forces at operational readiness for employment by the [Permanent Joint Headquarters](/wiki/Permanent_Joint_Headquarters "Permanent Joint Headquarters").[[13]](#cite_note-:3-14) The command structure is hierarchical, with overall command residing with the [Chief of the General Staff](/wiki/Chief_of_the_General_Staff_(United_Kingdom) "Chief of the General Staff (United Kingdom)") (CGS), who is immediately subordinate to The Chief of Defence Staff, the head of the British Armed Services. The CGS is supported by the [Deputy Chief of the General Staff](/wiki/Deputy_Chief_of_the_General_Staff_(United_Kingdom) "Deputy Chief of the General Staff (United Kingdom)"). Army Headquarters is further organised into two subordinate commands, [Field Army](/wiki/Field_Army_(United_Kingdom) "Field Army (United Kingdom)") and [Home Command](/wiki/Commander_Home_Command "Commander Home Command"), each commanded by a [lieutenant general](/wiki/Lieutenant-general_(United_Kingdom) "Lieutenant-general (United Kingdom)").[[197]](#cite_note-:4-198) These two Commands serve distinct purposes and are divided into a structure of [divisions](/wiki/Division_(military) "Division (military)") and [brigades](/wiki/Brigade "Brigade"), which themselves consist of a complex mix of smaller units such as [Battalions](/wiki/Battalion "Battalion"). British Army units are either full-time 'Regular' units, or part-time [Army Reserve](/wiki/Army_Reserve_(United_Kingdom) "Army Reserve (United Kingdom)") units.[[198]](#cite_note-199)

### Field Army

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Field Army")]

Main article: [Field Army (United Kingdom)](/wiki/Field_Army_(United_Kingdom) "Field Army (United Kingdom)")

Led by [Commander Field Army](/wiki/Commander_Field_Army "Commander Field Army"), the Field Army is responsible for generating and preparing forces for current and contingency operations. The Field Army comprises:[[197]](#cite_note-:4-198)

* [1st (United Kingdom) Division](/wiki/1st_(United_Kingdom)_Division "1st (United Kingdom) Division")
* [3rd (United Kingdom) Division](/wiki/3rd_(United_Kingdom)_Division "3rd (United Kingdom) Division")
* [Field Army Troops](/wiki/Field_Army_Troops "Field Army Troops")

### Home Command

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Home Command")]

Main article: [Home Command (British Army)](/wiki/Home_Command_(British_Army) "Home Command (British Army)")

Home Command is the British Army's supporting command; a generating, recruiting and training force that supports the Field Army and delivers UK resilience.[[197]](#cite_note-:4-198) It comprises

* Army Personnel Centre, which deals with personnel issues and liaises with outside agencies.[[200]](#cite_note-201)
* Army Personnel Services Group, which supports personnel administration[[197]](#cite_note-:4-198)
* [Army Recruiting and Initial Training Command](/wiki/Army_Recruiting_and_Initial_Training_Command "Army Recruiting and Initial Training Command"), which is responsible for all recruiting and training of Officers and Soldiers.[[197]](#cite_note-:4-198)
* [London District Command](/wiki/London_District_(British_Army) "London District (British Army)"), which is the main headquarters for all British Army units within the M25 corridor of London. It also provides for London's ceremonial events as well as supporting operational deployments overseas.[[201]](#cite_note-202)
* [Regional Command](/wiki/Regional_Command_(British_Army) "Regional Command (British Army)"), which enables the delivery of a secure home front that sustains the Army, notably helping to coordinate the British Army's support to the civil authorities, overseeing the British Army's Welfare Service, and delivering the British Army's civil engagement mission.[[202]](#cite_note-203)
* Standing Joint Command, which coordinates defence's contribution to UK resilience operations in support of other government departments.[[203]](#cite_note-204)

### Special Forces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Special Forces")]

[![Emblem of a winged sword with the motto, "Who dares, wins"](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/UK_SAS_%28badge%29.svg/250px-UK_SAS_%28badge%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:UK_SAS_(badge).svg)

SAS [cap badge](/wiki/Cap_badge "Cap badge")

The British Army contributes two of the three [special forces](/wiki/Special_forces "Special forces") formations to the [United Kingdom Special Forces](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Special_Forces "United Kingdom Special Forces") directorate: the [Special Air Service](/wiki/Special_Air_Service "Special Air Service") (SAS) and [Special Reconnaissance Regiment](/wiki/Special_Reconnaissance_Regiment "Special Reconnaissance Regiment") (SRR).[[204]](#cite_note-PPUK-205) The SAS consists of one regular and two reserve regiments.[[205]](#cite_note-206) The regular regiment, 22 SAS, has its headquarters at [Stirling Lines](/wiki/Stirling_Lines "Stirling Lines"), [Credenhill](/wiki/Credenhill "Credenhill"), [Herefordshire](/wiki/Herefordshire "Herefordshire"). It consists of 5 squadrons (A, B, D, G and Reserve) and a training wing.[[206]](#cite_note-207) 22 SAS is supported by 2 reserve regiments, [21 SAS](/wiki/Artists_Rifles "Artists Rifles") and 23 SAS, which collectively form the Special Air Service (Reserve) (SAS [R]), who in 2020 were transferred back under the command of Director of Special Forces after previously being under the command of the [1st Intelligence, Surveillance and Reconnaissance Brigade](/wiki/1st_Intelligence,_Surveillance_and_Reconnaissance_Brigade "1st Intelligence, Surveillance and Reconnaissance Brigade").[[207]](#cite_note-208) The SRR, formed in 2005, performs close reconnaissance and special surveillance tasks.[[204]](#cite_note-PPUK-205) The [Special Forces Support Group](/wiki/Special_Forces_Support_Group "Special Forces Support Group"), under the operational control of the Director of Special Forces, provides operational manoeuvring support to the United Kingdom Special Forces.[[208]](#cite_note-209)

When the [English Empire](/wiki/English_Empire "English Empire") was established in [North America](/wiki/British_America "British America") (including Bermuda), and the West Indies in the early 17th century there was no standing English Army, only the [Militia](/wiki/Militia_(English) "Militia (English)"), [Yeomanry](/wiki/Yeomanry "Yeomanry"), and [Royal bodyguards](/wiki/Sovereign%27s_Bodyguard "Sovereign's Bodyguard"), of which the Militia, as the primary home-defence force, was immediately extended to the colonies. [Colonial militias](/wiki/Militia_(British_Dominions_and_Crown_Colonies) "Militia (British Dominions and Crown Colonies)") defended colonies single-handedly at first against indigenous peoples and European competitors. Once the standing English Army, later the British Army, came into existence and began to garrison the colonies, the colonial militias fought side by side with it in a number of wars, including the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"). Some of the colonial militias rebelled during the [American War of Independence](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"). The militia fought alongside the regular British Army (and native allies) in defending [British North America](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America") from their former countrymen during the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812").[[209]](#cite_note-210)

Locally raised units in strategically located [Imperial fortress](/wiki/Imperial_fortress "Imperial fortress") colonies (including: [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") before the military defence, other than of the dockyard, passed to the new Dominion Government on [Canadian Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation") and the Royal Naval Dockyard there was transferred to the Dominion Government in 1905; [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda") – which was treated as part of [The Maritimes](/wiki/The_Maritimes "The Maritimes") under the [Commander-in-Chief at Nova Scotia](/wiki/Commander-in-Chief,_North_America#Commanders-in-Chief,_maritime_provinces,_1783–1875 "Commander-in-Chief, North America") until the [Confederation of Canada](/wiki/Confederation_of_Canada "Confederation of Canada"), with its garrison, formerly commanded by a Major-General, becoming the [Bermuda Command](/wiki/Bermuda_Garrison "Bermuda Garrison") thereafter, under a General or Lieutenant General as [General Officer Commanding-in-Chief](/wiki/General_Officer_Commanding "General Officer Commanding"); [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar"); and [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta")) and the [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands") were generally maintained from army funds and more fully integrated into the British Army as evident from their placements in British Army lists, unlike units such as the [King's African Rifles](/wiki/King%27s_African_Rifles "King's African Rifles").[[210]](#cite_note-211)

The larger colonies (Australia, New Zealand, Canada, South Africa, etc.) mostly achieved [Commonwealth Dominion](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion") status before or after the First World War and were granted full legislative independence in 1931. While remaining within the British Empire, this placed their governments on a par with the British government, and hence their military units comprised separate armies (e.g. the [Australian Army](/wiki/Australian_Army "Australian Army")), although Canada retained the term "militia" for its military forces until the Second World War. From the 1940s, these dominions and many colonies chose full independence, usually becoming [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm") (as member states of the Commonwealth are known today).[[211]](#cite_note-212)[[212]](#cite_note-213)

Units raised in [self-governing](/wiki/Self-governing_colony "Self-governing colony") and [Crown colonies](/wiki/Crown_colony "Crown colony") (those without local elected [Legislatures](/wiki/Legislatures "Legislatures"), as was the case with [British Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong")) that are part of the British realm remain under British Government control. As the territorial governments are delegated responsibility only for internal government, the UK Government, as the government of the [Sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state"), retains responsibility for national security and the defence of the fourteen remaining [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories"),[[213]](#cite_note-214) of which six have locally raised regiments:

* [Royal Bermuda Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Bermuda_Regiment "Royal Bermuda Regiment")[[214]](#cite_note-215)
* [Royal Gibraltar Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Gibraltar_Regiment "Royal Gibraltar Regiment")[[215]](#cite_note-216)
* [Falkland Islands Defence Force](/wiki/Falkland_Islands_Defence_Force "Falkland Islands Defence Force")[[216]](#cite_note-217)
* [Royal Montserrat Defence Force](/wiki/Royal_Montserrat_Defence_Force "Royal Montserrat Defence Force")[[217]](#cite_note-218)
* [Cayman Islands Regiment](/wiki/Cayman_Islands_Regiment "Cayman Islands Regiment")[[218]](#cite_note-219)
* [Turks and Caicos Islands Regiment](/wiki/Turks_and_Caicos_Islands_Regiment "Turks and Caicos Islands Regiment")[[219]](#cite_note-220)

### Levels of Command

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Levels of Command")]

The structure of the British Army beneath the level of Divisions and Brigades is also hierarchical and command is based on rank. The table below details how many units within the British Army are structured, although there can be considerable variation between individual units:[[197]](#cite_note-:4-198)

| Type of unit | [Division](/wiki/Division_(military) "Division (military)") | [Brigade](/wiki/Brigade "Brigade") | [Battlegroup](/wiki/Battlegroup_(army) "Battlegroup (army)") | [Battalion](/wiki/Battalion "Battalion"), [Regiment](/wiki/Regiment "Regiment") | [Company](/wiki/Company_(military_unit) "Company (military unit)"), [Squadron](/wiki/Squadron_(army) "Squadron (army)"), [Battery](/wiki/Artillery_battery "Artillery battery") | [Platoon](/wiki/Platoon "Platoon") or [Troop](/wiki/Troop "Troop") | [Section](/wiki/Section_(military_unit) "Section (military unit)") | [Fire team](/wiki/Fireteam "Fireteam") |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Contains | 3 brigades | 3–5 battalions (battlegroups) | Combined arms unit | 4–6 companies | 3 platoons | 3 sections | 2 fire teams | 4 individuals |
| Personnel | 10,000 | 5,000 | 700–1,000 | 720 | 120 | 30 | 8–10 | 4 |
| Commanded by | [Maj-Gen](/wiki/Major_General_(United_Kingdom) "Major General (United Kingdom)") | [Brig](/wiki/Brigadier_(United_Kingdom) "Brigadier (United Kingdom)") | [Lt Col](/wiki/Lieutenant_Colonel_(United_Kingdom) "Lieutenant Colonel (United Kingdom)") | [Lt Col](/wiki/Lieutenant_colonel "Lieutenant colonel") | [Major](/wiki/Major_(United_Kingdom) "Major (United Kingdom)") | [Lt](/wiki/Lieutenant "Lieutenant") or [2nd Lt](/wiki/2nd_Lieutenant#United_Kingdom_and_Commonwealth "2nd Lieutenant") | [Cpl](/wiki/Corporal#United_Kingdom "Corporal") | [LCpl](/wiki/Lance_corporal#United_Kingdom "Lance corporal") |

Whilst many units are organised as Battalions or Regiments administratively, the most common fighting unit is the combined arms unit known as a Battlegroup. This is formed around a combat unit and supported by units (or sub-units) from other capabilities. An example of a battlegroup would be two companies of armoured infantry (e.g. from the 1st Battalion of the [Mercian Regiment](/wiki/Mercian_Regiment "Mercian Regiment")), one squadron of heavy armour (e.g. A Squadron of the [Royal Tank Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Tank_Regiment "Royal Tank Regiment")), a company of engineers (e.g. B Company of the 22nd Engineer Regiment), a Battery of artillery (e.g. D Battery of the 1st Regiment of the [Royal Horse Artillery](/wiki/Royal_Horse_Artillery "Royal Horse Artillery")) and smaller attachments from medical, logistic and intelligence units. Typically organised and commanded by a battlegroup headquarters and named after the unit which provided the most combat units, in this example, it would be the 1 Mercian Battlegroup. This creates a self-sustaining mixed formation of armour, infantry, artillery, engineers and support units, commanded by a lieutenant colonel.[[221]](#cite_note-SAS-222)

## Recruitment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Recruitment")]

Main article: [Recruitment in the British Army](/wiki/Recruitment_in_the_British_Army "Recruitment in the British Army")

[![World War I recruiting poster, with Lord Kitchener pointing at the viewer](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/30a_Sammlung_Eybl_Gro%C3%9Fbritannien._Alfred_Leete_%281882%E2%80%931933%29_Britons_%28Kitchener%29_wants_you_%28Briten_Kitchener_braucht_Euch%29._1914_%28Nachdruck%29%2C_74_x_50_cm._%28Slg.Nr._552%29.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:30a_Sammlung_Eybl_Gro%C3%9Fbritannien._Alfred_Leete_(1882%E2%80%931933)_Britons_(Kitchener)_wants_you_(Briten_Kitchener_braucht_Euch)._1914_(Nachdruck),_74_x_50_cm._(Slg.Nr._552).jpg)

One of the most recognisable recruiting posters of the British Army; from [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), with [Lord Kitchener](/wiki/Herbert_Kitchener,_1st_Earl_Kitchener "Herbert Kitchener, 1st Earl Kitchener")

The British Army primarily recruits from within the United Kingdom, but accept applications from all British citizens. It also accepts applications from Irish citizens and [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") citizens, with certain restrictions.[[222]](#cite_note-:5-223) Since 2018 the British Army has been an equal-opportunity employer (with some legal exceptions due to medical standards), and does not discriminate based on race, religion or sexual orientation.[[223]](#cite_note-224) Applicants for the Regular Army must be a minimum age of 16, although soldiers under 18 may not serve in operations, and the maximum age is 36. Applicants for the Army Reserve must be a minimum of 17 years and 9 months, and a maximum age of 43. Different age limits apply for Officers and those in some specialist roles. Applicants must also meet several other requirements, notably regarding medical health, physical fitness, past-criminal convictions, education, and regarding any tattoos and piercings.[[222]](#cite_note-:5-223)

Soldiers and officers in the Regular Army now enlist for an initial period of 12 years, with options to extend if they meet certain requirements. Soldiers and officers are normally required to serve for a minimum of 4 years from date of enlistment and must give 12 months' notice before leaving; soldiers who joined before the age of 18 years old are normally required to serve for a minimum of 6 years.[[224]](#cite_note-225)

### Oath of allegiance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Oath of allegiance")]

All soldiers and commissioned officers must take an oath of allegiance upon joining the Army, a process known as attestation. Those who wish to swear by [God](/wiki/God_in_Abrahamic_religions "God in Abrahamic religions") use the following words:[[12]](#cite_note-:2-13)

> I, [soldier's or commissioned officer's name], swear by Almighty God that I will be faithful and bear true allegiance to [His Majesty King Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"), his heirs and successors and that I will as in duty bound honestly and faithfully defend His Majesty, his heirs and successors in person, [crown](/wiki/British_Crown "British Crown") and dignity against all enemies and will observe and obey all orders of His Majesty, his heirs, and successors and of the generals and officers set over me.[[225]](#cite_note-226)

Others replace the words "swear by Almighty God" with "solemnly, sincerely and truly declare and affirm".[[12]](#cite_note-:2-13)

### Training

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Training")]

[![Red-brick buildings with large windows](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/RMAS18Je6-4685.jpg/250px-RMAS18Je6-4685.jpg)](/wiki/File:RMAS18Je6-4685.jpg)

New College buildings at Royal Military Academy Sandhurst

See also: [Selection and Training in the British Army](/wiki/Selection_and_Training_in_the_British_Army "Selection and Training in the British Army") and [List of British Army installations](/wiki/List_of_British_Army_installations "List of British Army installations")

Candidates for the Army undergo common training, beginning with [initial military training](/wiki/Recruit_training "Recruit training"), to bring all personnel to a similar standard in basic military skills, which is known as Phase 1 training. They then undertake further specialist trade-training for their specific Regiment or Corps, known as Phase 2 training. After completing Phase 1 training a soldier is counted against the Army's trained strength, and upon completion of Phase 2 are counted against the Army's fully trained trade strength.[[226]](#cite_note-boot-227)

Soldiers under the age of 17 and six months will complete Phase 1 training at the [Army Foundation College](/wiki/Army_Foundation_College "Army Foundation College").[[227]](#cite_note-228) Infantry Soldiers will complete combined Phase 1 & 2 training at the [Infantry Training Centre, Catterick](/wiki/Infantry_Training_Centre_(British_Army) "Infantry Training Centre (British Army)"), whilst all other Soldiers will attend Phase 1 training at the [Army Training Centre Pirbright](/wiki/Army_Training_Centre_Pirbright "Army Training Centre Pirbright") or [Army Training Regiment, Winchester](/wiki/Army_training_regiment "Army training regiment"), and then complete Phase 2 training at different locations depending on their specialism.[[226]](#cite_note-boot-227) Officers conduct their initial training at the [Royal Military Academy Sandhurst](/wiki/Royal_Military_Academy_Sandhurst "Royal Military Academy Sandhurst") (RMAS),[[228]](#cite_note-229) before also completing their Phase 2 training at multiple different locations.[[226]](#cite_note-boot-227)

## Flags and ensigns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Flags and ensigns")]

See also: [Regulation Colours](/wiki/Regulation_Colours "Regulation Colours")

The British Army's official flag is the [Union Jack](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack"). The Army also has a non-ceremonial flag that is often seen flying from military buildings and is used at recruiting and military events and exhibitions.[[229]](#cite_note-230) Traditionally most British Army units had a set of flags, known as the [colours](/wiki/Colours,_standards_and_guidons "Colours, standards and guidons")—normally a Regimental Colour and a King's Colour (the Union Jack). Historically these were carried into battle as a rallying point for the soldiers and were closely guarded. In modern units the colours are often prominently displayed, decorated with [battle honours](/wiki/Battle_honours_of_the_British_and_Imperial_Armies "Battle honours of the British and Imperial Armies"), and act as a focal point for Regimental pride.[[230]](#cite_note-231) A soldier re-joining a regiment (upon recall from the reserve) is described as *re-called to the Colours*.[[231]](#cite_note-232)

## Reserve forces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Reserve forces")]

Main article: [Army Reserve (United Kingdom)](/wiki/Army_Reserve_(United_Kingdom) "Army Reserve (United Kingdom)")

The oldest of the Reserve Forces was the [Militia Force](/wiki/Militia_(Great_Britain) "Militia (Great Britain)") (also referred to as the *Constitutional Force*),[[241]](#cite_note-248)[[242]](#cite_note-249)[[243]](#cite_note-250)[[244]](#cite_note-251) which (in the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), prior to 1707) was originally the main military defensive force (until the 1645 creation of the [New Model Army](/wiki/New_Model_Army "New Model Army"), there otherwise were originally only Royal bodyguards, including the [Yeomen Warders](/wiki/Yeomen_Warders "Yeomen Warders") and the [Yeomen of the Guard](/wiki/Yeomen_of_the_Guard "Yeomen of the Guard"), with armies raised only temporarily for expeditions overseas), made up of civilians embodied for annual training or emergencies, which had used various schemes of compulsory service during different periods of its long existence. From the 1850s it recruited volunteers who engaged for terms of service. The Militia was originally an all-infantry force, though [Militia coastal artillery](/wiki/Militia_Artillery_units_of_the_United_Kingdom_and_Colonies "Militia Artillery units of the United Kingdom and Colonies"), field artillery, and engineers units were introduced from the 1850s.[[245]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-252)

[Volunteer Force](/wiki/Volunteer_Force "Volunteer Force") units were also frequently raised during wartime and disbanded upon peace. This was re-established as a permanent (i.e., in war and peace) part of the Reserve Forces in 1859. It differed from the Militia in a number of ways, most particularly in that volunteers did not commit to a term service, and were able to resign with fourteen days notice (except while embodied). As volunteer soldiers were originally expected to fund the cost of their own equipment, few tended to come from the labouring class among whom the Militia primarily recruited.[[246]](#cite_note-253)[[247]](#cite_note-254)

The [Yeomanry Force](/wiki/Yeomanry "Yeomanry") was made up of mounted units, organised similarly to the Volunteer Force, first raised during the two decades of war with France that followed the French Revolution. As with the Volunteers, members of the Yeomanry were expected to foot much of the cost of their own equipment, including their horses, and the make-up of the units tended to be from more affluent classes.[[248]](#cite_note-255)[[249]](#cite_note-256)

Although Militia regiments were linked with British Army regiments during the course of the Napoleonic Wars to feed volunteers for service abroad into the regular army, and volunteers from the Reserve Forces served abroad either individually or in contingents, service companies, or battalions in a succession of conflicts from the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War") to the [Second Boer War](/wiki/Second_Boer_War "Second Boer War"), personnel did not normally move between forces unless re-attested as a member of the new force, and units did not normally move from the Reserve Forces to become part of the Regular Forces, or vice versa. There were exceptions, however, as with the *New Brunswick Regiment of Fencible Infantry*, raised in 1803, which became the [104th (New Brunswick) Regiment of Foot](/wiki/104th_(New_Brunswick)_Regiment_of_Foot "104th (New Brunswick) Regiment of Foot") when it was transferred to the British Army on 13 September 1810.[[250]](#cite_note-257)

Another type of reserve force was created during the period between the French Revolution and the end of the Napoleonic Wars. Called [Fencibles](/wiki/Fencibles "Fencibles"), these were disbanded after the Napoleonic Wars and not raised again, although the [Royal Malta Fencible Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Malta_Fencible_Regiment "Royal Malta Fencible Regiment"), later the *Royal Malta Fencible Artillery*, existed from 1815 until the 1880s when it became the [Royal Malta Artillery](/wiki/Royal_Malta_Artillery "Royal Malta Artillery"),[[251]](#cite_note-258) and the [Royal New Zealand Fencible Corps](/wiki/Royal_New_Zealand_Fencible_Corps "Royal New Zealand Fencible Corps") was formed in 1846.[[252]](#cite_note-259)[[253]](#cite_note-260)

The Reserve Forces were raised locally (in Britain, under the control of [Lords-Lieutenant](/wiki/Lord-Lieutenant "Lord-Lieutenant") of counties, and, in [British colonies](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories"), under the [colonial governors](/wiki/Governor#United_Kingdom_overseas_territories "Governor"), and members originally were obliged to serve only within their locality (which, in the United Kingdom, originally meant within the county or other recruitment area, but was extended to anywhere in Britain, though not overseas). They have consequently also been referred to as *Local Forces*. As they were (and in some cases *are*) considered separate forces from the British Army, though still within the British military, they have also been known as *Auxiliary Forces*. The Militia and Volunteer units of a colony were generally considered to be separate forces from the *Home* Militia Force and Volunteer Force in the United Kingdom, and from the Militia Forces and Volunteer Forces of other colonies. Where a colony had more than one Militia or Volunteer unit, they would be grouped as a Militia or Volunteer Force for that colony, such as the Jamaica Volunteer Defence Force. Officers of the Reserve Forces could not sit on Courts Martial of regular forces personnel. The [Mutiny Act](/wiki/Mutiny_Act "Mutiny Act") did not apply to members of the Reserve Forces. The *Reserve Forces* within the British Isles were increasingly integrated with the British Army through a succession of reforms (beginning with the [Cardwell Reforms](/wiki/Cardwell_Reforms "Cardwell Reforms")) of the British military forces over the last two decades of the Nineteenth Century and the early years of the Twentieth Century, whereby the Reserve Forces units mostly lost their own identities and became numbered Militia or Volunteer battalions of regular British Army corps or regiments.[[254]](#cite_note-261)

In 1908, the Yeomanry and Volunteer Force were merged to create the [Territorial Force](/wiki/Territorial_Force "Territorial Force") (changed to *Territorial Army* after the First World War), with terms of service similar to the army and Militia, and the Militia was renamed the *Special Reserve*,[[255]](#cite_note-262)[[256]](#cite_note-263)[[257]](#cite_note-264) After the First World War the Special Reserve was renamed the Militia, again, but permanently suspended (although a handful of Militia units survived in the United Kingdom, its colonies, and the Crown Dependencies). Although the Territorial Force was nominally still a separate force from the British Army, by the end of the century, at the latest, any unit wholly or partly funded from Army Funds was considered part of the British Army. Outside the United Kingdom-proper, this was generally only the case for those units in the [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands") or the [Imperial fortress](/wiki/Imperial_fortress "Imperial fortress") colonies ([Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), before [Canadian confederation](/wiki/Canadian_confederation "Canadian confederation"); [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda"); [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar"); and [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta")).[[258]](#cite_note-265)[[259]](#cite_note-266)[[260]](#cite_note-267)

The [Bermuda Militia Artillery](/wiki/Bermuda_Militia_Artillery "Bermuda Militia Artillery"), [Bermuda Militia Infantry](/wiki/Bermuda_Militia_Infantry "Bermuda Militia Infantry"), [Bermuda Volunteer Engineers](/wiki/Bermuda_Volunteer_Engineers "Bermuda Volunteer Engineers"), and the [Bermuda Volunteer Rifle Corps](/wiki/Bermuda_Volunteer_Rifle_Corps "Bermuda Volunteer Rifle Corps"),[[261]](#cite_note-268)[[245]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-252) by example were paid for by the War Office and considered part of the British Army, with their officers appearing as such in the *Army List* unlike those of many other colonial units deemed auxiliaries. Today, the British Army is the only Home British military force, including the various other forces it has absorbed, though British military units organised on Territorial Army lines remain in British Overseas Territories that are still not considered formally part of the British Army, with only the [Royal Gibraltar Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Gibraltar_Regiment "Royal Gibraltar Regiment") and the [Royal Bermuda Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Bermuda_Regiment "Royal Bermuda Regiment") (an amalgam of the old Bermuda Militia Artillery and Bermuda Volunteer Rifle Corps) appearing on the [British Army order-of-precedence](/wiki/British_Army_order_of_precedence "British Army order of precedence") and in the [Army List](/wiki/Army_List "Army List"), as well as on the [Corps Warrant](/wiki/Corps_Warrant "Corps Warrant") (the official list of those British military forces that are considered corps of the British Army).[[262]](#cite_note-269)[[263]](#cite_note-270)[[264]](#cite_note-271)[[265]](#cite_note-272)[[266]](#cite_note-273)[[267]](#cite_note-274)[[268]](#cite_note-275)[[269]](#cite_note-276)

In October 2012 the Ministry of Defence announced that the Territorial Army was to be renamed the Army Reserve.[[270]](#cite_note-277)

## Uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Army&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Uniforms")]

Further information: [Uniforms of the British Army](/wiki/Uniforms_of_the_British_Army "Uniforms of the British Army")

The British Army uniform has sixteen categories, ranging from ceremonial uniforms to combat dress to evening wear. No. 8 Dress, the day-to-day uniform, is known as "Personal Clothing System – Combat Uniform" (PCS-CU)[[271]](#cite_note-278) and consists of a [Multi-Terrain Pattern](/wiki/Multi-Terrain_Pattern "Multi-Terrain Pattern") (MTP) windproof smock, a lightweight jacket and trousers with ancillary items such as [thermals](/wiki/Long_underwear "Long underwear") and [waterproofs](/wiki/Raincoat "Raincoat").[[272]](#cite_note-dresscodes-279) The army has introduced [tactical recognition flashes](/wiki/Tactical_recognition_flash "Tactical recognition flash") (TRFs); worn on the right arm of a combat uniform, the insignia denotes the wearer's regiment or corps.[[273]](#cite_note-280) In addition to working dress, the army has a number of parade uniforms for ceremonial and non-ceremonial occasions. The most-commonly-seen uniforms are No. 1 Dress (full ceremonial, seen at formal occasions such as at the changing of the guard at Buckingham Palace) and No. 2 Dress (Service Dress), a brown [khaki](/wiki/Khaki "Khaki") uniform worn for non-ceremonial parades.[[272]](#cite_note-dresscodes-279)[[274]](#cite_note-281)

Working [headdress](/wiki/Headgear "Headgear") is typically a [beret](/wiki/Beret "Beret"), whose colour indicates its wearer's type of regiment. Beret colours include:[[275]](#cite_note-282)

* Khaki—[Foot Guards](/wiki/Foot_Guards "Foot Guards"), [Honourable Artillery Company](/wiki/Honourable_Artillery_Company "Honourable Artillery Company"), [Princess of Wales's Royal Regiment](/wiki/Princess_of_Wales%27s_Royal_Regiment "Princess of Wales's Royal Regiment"), [Royal Anglian Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Anglian_Regiment "Royal Anglian Regiment"), [Royal Welsh](/wiki/Royal_Welsh "Royal Welsh"), [Royal Yorkshire Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Yorkshire_Regiment "Royal Yorkshire Regiment")
* Light grey—[Royal Scots Dragoon Guards](/wiki/Royal_Scots_Dragoon_Guards "Royal Scots Dragoon Guards")
* Brown—[King's Royal Hussars](/wiki/King%27s_Royal_Hussars "King's Royal Hussars"), [Royal Wessex Yeomanry](/wiki/Royal_Wessex_Yeomanry "Royal Wessex Yeomanry")
* Black—[Royal Tank Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Tank_Regiment "Royal Tank Regiment")
* Dark ([rifle](/wiki/Rifle_green "Rifle green")) green—[Royal Dragoon Guards](/wiki/Royal_Dragoon_Guards "Royal Dragoon Guards"), [The Rifles](/wiki/The_Rifles "The Rifles"), [Royal Gurkha Rifles](/wiki/Royal_Gurkha_Rifles "Royal Gurkha Rifles"), [Small Arms School Corps](/wiki/Small_Arms_School_Corps "Small Arms School Corps")
* [Maroon](/wiki/Maroon_beret "Maroon beret")—[Parachute Regiment](/wiki/Parachute_Regiment_(United_Kingdom) "Parachute Regiment (United Kingdom)")
* Beige—[Special Air Service](/wiki/Special_Air_Service "Special Air Service")
* Sky blue—[Army Air Corps](/wiki/Army_Air_Corps_(United_Kingdom) "Army Air Corps (United Kingdom)")
* Cypress green—[Intelligence Corps](/wiki/Intelligence_Corps_(United_Kingdom) "Intelligence Corps (United Kingdom)")
* Scarlet—[Royal Military Police](/wiki/Royal_Military_Police "Royal Military Police")
* Green—[Adjutant General's Corps](/wiki/Adjutant_General%27s_Corps "Adjutant General's Corps")
* Navy blue—All other units, such as the [Household Cavalry](/wiki/Household_Cavalry "Household Cavalry"), [Light Dragoons](/wiki/Light_Dragoons "Light Dragoons"), [Queen's Dragoon Guards](/wiki/Queen%27s_Dragoon_Guards "Queen's Dragoon Guards"), [Royal Yeomanry](/wiki/Royal_Yeomanry "Royal Yeomanry"), and the [Royal Regiment of Fusiliers](/wiki/Royal_Regiment_of_Fusiliers "Royal Regiment of Fusiliers")
* Emerald grey—[Special Reconnaissance Regiment](/wiki/Special_Reconnaissance_Regiment "Special Reconnaissance Regiment")
* Gun-metal grey—[The Ranger Regiment](/wiki/Ranger_Regiment_(United_Kingdom) "Ranger Regiment (United Kingdom)")[[276]](#cite_note-283)
